coredata.dc:description: As awareness of environmental protection becomes increasingly pronounced, functional sustainable packaging becomes more important in food applications. In this study, a multifunctional intelligent/active package was developed using pectin extracted from sponge gourd (Luffa cylindrica L.) peel as a film matrix combined with sponge gourd peel-derived ZnO-doped carbon dots (Zn–CD) and purple potato extract (PE). PE and Zn–CD synergistically altered the microstructure of the pectin film (PC), increasing its barrier properties, mechanical performance, antioxidant activity, antibacterial properties, UV resistance, pH color responsiveness, anticounterfeiting capabilities, and biodegradability. PC containing 5 wt% PE and 3 wt% Zn–CD (PC-5%PE-CD) has strong pH sensitivity and can be used to determine milk and beef freshness based on changes in film color. PC-5%PE-CD packaging is effective in prolonging blueberry freshness. In addition, compared with polyethylene film, PC-5%PE-CD was not only easily degradable in soil but also capable of regulating soil microbial communities, providing nutrients for Brassica rapa L., promoting its growth, and enhancing the sustainability of resources. Therefore, the PC-5%PE-CD composite can serve as a potential multifunctional and environmentally friendly sustainable food packaging material.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As awareness of environmental protection becomes increasingly pronounced, functional sustainable packaging becomes more important in food applications. In this study, a multifunctional intelligent/active package was developed using pectin extracted from sponge gourd (Luffa cylindrica L.) peel as a film matrix combined with sponge gourd peel-derived ZnO-doped carbon dots (Zn–CD) and purple potato extract (PE). PE and Zn–CD synergistically altered the microstructure of the pectin film (PC), increasing its barrier properties, mechanical performance, antioxidant activity, antibacterial properties, UV resistance, pH color responsiveness, anticounterfeiting capabilities, and biodegradability. PC containing 5 wt% PE and 3 wt% Zn–CD (PC-5%PE-CD) has strong pH sensitivity and can be used to determine milk and beef freshness based on changes in film color. PC-5%PE-CD packaging is effective in prolonging blueberry freshness. In addition, compared with polyethylene film, PC-5%PE-CD was not only easily degradable in soil but also capable of regulating soil microbial communities, providing nutrients for Brassica rapa L., promoting its growth, and enhancing the sustainability of resources. Therefore, the PC-5%PE-CD composite can serve as a potential multifunctional and environmentally friendly sustainable food packaging material.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Climate change is among the most urgent challenges today, presenting a significant danger to human society, and addressing global warming demands a comprehensive, multilevel strategy [1]. Polyethylene disposal is a major contributor to greenhouse gas emissions and environmental degradation [2]. Excessive reliance on polyethylene packaging alone accounts for approximately 46 % of global polyethylene waste, posing a substantial threat to both ecosystems and human health [3]. Consequently, creating biodegradable materials for food packaging has become especially urgent to address this issue [4]. Pectin stands out as a promising biodegradable material owing to its nontoxicity, edibility, ease of extraction, good gelatinization properties, safety, biocompatibility, and renewability [5]. However, pure pectin film (PC) has encountered obstacles, such as elevated water vapor permeability and limited structural strength [6]. Therefore, the development of biodegradable PC-based packaging films that integrate antibacterial, antioxidant, anti-UV, freshness preservation, freshness monitoring, and anticounterfeiting functions is expected to overcome these challenges [3].

Sponge gourd (Luffa cylindrica L.) is a dicotyledonous vine plant that belongs to the Cucurbitaceae family. Originating in tropical Asia, it has become a valuable vegetable in tropical and subtropical regions around the world [7]. However, sponge gourd peel (SGP), which contains an abundance of minerals and dietary fiber, is frequently discarded. The cell wall and intercellular layer of SGP contain a large amount of pectin, which is recognized for its gelling ability [8]. Although commercial pectin is typically extracted from citrus peels and apple pomace, recent research has explored a wider range of fruits and agricultural byproducts as alternative sources of pectin [6]. These include carrot residues, hawthorn, tomatoes, sunflower seeds, and pumpkin residues [5]. These results suggest that SGP also holds great potential for pectin production. Moreover, the waste residues generated during the extraction process have high potential as a carbon source for utilization.

Carbon dots (CD), as a type of carbon-based nanomaterial, have attracted considerable interest because of their outstanding physicochemical attributes [1]. CD are produced from various carbon sources using the hydrothermal method, and they exhibit characteristics such as size control, functional group diversification, fluorescence sensing, potent antibacterial and antioxidant activity, good biocompatibility, and excellent water solubility [9]. These attributes make them highly suitable for applications in the packaging industry. However, pure CD tend to aggregate, which can negatively impact their performance [10]. To address this, doping with metal nanoparticles, such as ZnO, is an effective strategy to increase their stability and functionality. The incorporation of ZnO-doped CD (Zn–CD) can significantly increase the compactness, along with the antimicrobial and antioxidant capabilities of the films [9]. However, pectin films with Zn–CD have high fracture properties, whereas the addition of glycerol effectively improves their flexibility [11]. Despite these considerations, the synthesis of Zn–CD from the filter residue of SGP and its potential effects on the properties of pectin-based films remain largely unexplored and warrant further investigation.

Anthocyanins, which are natural pigments that dissolve in water and occur in various fruits and vegetables, are notable for their vivid shifts in hue across a wide pH range [12]. Recently, researchers have developed intelligent indicator films using anthocyanins extracted from sources such as butterfly pea flowers, purple kale, and purple carrots [9,10,13]. Purple potatoes, which are rich in anthocyanins (approximately 9.5 to 40 mg per 100 g of fresh material) [14], display a broad spectrum of color (from pink to green to yellowish-brown) depending on the pH, aiding in pH identification. Therefore, the use of anthocyanin-rich purple potatoes to develop pH-sensitive intelligent films represents a potential approach for tracking the freshness of food. Moreover, in a previous study, Khalifa et al. used purple potato peel as an intelligent indicator material to monitor the spoilage of Barramundi fish [15].

The goal of this study was to develop multifunctional intelligent/active packaging (Fig. 1 ) using pectin extracted from waste SGP as a film-forming matrix in combination with SGP-derived Zn–CD and purple potato extract (PE) to determine the freshness of milk, meat and blueberry preservation. In addition, this study investigated the role and structure of PE and Zn–CD and their effects on the barrier and mechanical properties, microstructure, antimicrobial and antioxidant activities, pH responsiveness, biodegradability, and soil microbes of the films. The innovation of this study is the conversion of waste SGP into a valuable film matrix to make an environmentally friendly and sustainable PC–PE–CD film, which not only prolongs the food preservation period and intelligently monitors freshness but also helps plant growth after degradation, realizing sustainable resource utilization and environmental protection. Moreover, it has excellent physicochemical properties, with significantly improved stability, biodegradability and UV protection.

Glycerol (MW = 92.09, purity ≥ 99 %), 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), citric acid (purity ≥ 99.5 %, MW = 192.12) and zinc oxide (nanopowder, 30 ± 10 nm, purity ≥ 99.9 %) were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sponge gourd, beef, salmon, blueberries and milk were obtained from a local market in Hefei, China. The Milk Freshness Rapid Test Kit was sourced from Wuxi Yuxiang Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Wuxi, China). All other chemicals and reagents employed were of analytical grade.

The Firdaus et al. method was used to extract pectin from SGP [5]. Pectin was extracted from SGP via the acidic solvent method. The SGP was thoroughly cleaned, sliced into small pieces, and dehydrated in a hot air oven (60 °C, 24 h). Once dried, the SGP was ground using a blender and sieved through an 80-mesh screen. To prepare the extraction mixture, 4 g of SGP powder was mixed with 100 mL of 1 M citric acid solution and stirred continuously for 90 min at 80 °C. The powder was extracted and passed through a filter. Following extraction, the mixture was filtered through a cloth and then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min. Subsequently, 96 % ethanol was added to the aqueous extract, and the mixture was left to settle in the refrigerator (4 °C) for approximately 18 h. The resulting pectin precipitate was collected and rinsed three times with 96 % ethanol to eliminate impurities. Finally, the precipitate was dehydrated in a hot air oven (50 °C, 24 h) and then stored at 4 °C for later use.
The characterization methods for pectin are described in the Supporting Information.

The method from Xie et al. was used to extract PE from purple potato [16]. The purple potatoes were first washed, freeze-dried, and ground into a fine powder. This powder was then sieved using a 60-mesh screen, and 10 g of the sifted powder was combined with 100 mL of an 80 % ethanol solution (v/v) containing 1 % HCl (v/v). The mixture was gently agitated every 2 h and left to stand at 4 °C for 24 h. The mixture was subsequently centrifuged for 10 min at 5000 rpm, after which the solution was passed through filter paper. No other solvents were used in the centrifugation or filtration processes. The resulting extract was then concentrated at 60 °C. Finally, the PE was freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C for future use.

The Folin–Ciocalteu assay method was used to measure the total phenolic content (TPC) of PE, with results expressed in milligrams of gallic acid (GA) per gram of extract. All measurements were performed in triplicate to ensure accuracy. The anthocyanin content in PE was assessed through the pH difference method [12]. To determine the absorbance, a 1 mg/mL PE solution was prepared, and readings were taken at 700 nm and 520 nm by dissolving the PE solution in buffer solutions at pH values of 4.5 and 1, respectively. After appropriate dilution, a 1 mL aliquot of the PE solution (1 mg/mL) was introduced into buffer agents of varying pH (3–12) for 3 min each, with photographs taken to capture color changes. The absorption spectra of PE in different buffer agents were then measured using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Lambda 35, PerkinElmer, America) across a range of wavelengths from 450 to 700 nm.

Following the extraction of pectin, the filtered residue was dried and repurposed as a carbon source for CD production, with Zn–CD produced in a subsequent step [17]. In summary, 1.0 g of the SGP powder filtrate was combined with ZnO nanoparticles (100 mg) in 60 mL of distilled water. The mixture was stirred for ten minutes before being transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL) and heated (190 °C, 6 h). After heating, the resulting yellowish-brown solution was left to cool to 25 °C and then filtered (100 μm pore size). The mixture was subsequently centrifuged (10000 rpm, 30 min) to remove any unreacted ZnO or residual particles. To further purify the ZnO-doped CD (Zn–CD), the supernatant was filtered through a Whatman membrane (0.22 μm pore size, 25 mm diameter). The final Zn–CD solution, which was brown–yellow in color, was stored at 4 °C for future use.

The microstructure of Zn–CD was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, HT7700, Hitachi Production Co., Ltd., Japan) [1]. A fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-2710, Hitachi, Japan) and a UV–Vis spectrophotometer were used to assess the fluorescence response and absorption spectra, respectively. The elemental composition and functional groups present in Zn–CD were identified through Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Nicolet iS50, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) across the range of 4000–500 cm−1. Additionally, the elemental makeup was analyzed via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Panalytical Empyrean, PANalytical B.V., Holland).

PC-based films were created via the method described by Khan et al. [9], with some modifications. First, 2 g of pectin (2 %, w/v) was dissolved in 100 mL of distilled water and stirred at 80 °C for 2 h. Glycerol, at 30 % of the polymer mass, was added, followed by the incorporation of 3 wt% and 5 wt% PE, with vigorous stirring at 40 °C for 1 h. Our tests revealed that the film with 5 wt% PE demonstrated superior properties, so this concentration was selected for the final composite film preparation. Then, 3 wt% Zn–CD was added to the 5 wt% PE solution and mixed for 30 min. The film-forming solution was degassed via ultrasonication, and the solution (30 g) was poured into a Petri dish (9 cm) and dried at 40 °C for 6 h. The films were subsequently stored at 25 °C and 75 % RH for 48 h. Different PE formulations (0–5 wt%) were designated PC, PC-3%PE, and PC-5%PE, while the films containing Zn–CD were labeled PC-CD and PC-5%PE-CD, respectively.

The cross-sections and surface morphologies of the films were analyzed by SEM (S-4800, Hitachi Production Co., Ltd., Japan). Additionally, the surface elemental composition was analyzed through energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS, Ametek, USA) [1].
The interactions between various film components were examined via an FT-IR spectrometer, covering a spectral range of 4000–500 cm−1 [18].
The XRD patterns of the film samples were obtained using an X-ray diffractometer (D8 Advance, Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany).
The thermal properties of the samples were evaluated using a thermogravimetric analyzer (SDT 650, TA, USA).

The transmittance of the films was determined using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. To visually evaluate film clarity, samples were placed over white paper featuring cartoon images and photographed with a digital camera. The color parameters (b*, L*, a*, ΔE) were measured with a colorimeter (PS2020, Sanenshi Intelligent Technology Co., Ltd., China) [18]. The anticounterfeiting effect was evaluated at a wavelength of 365 nm under UV light. Additionally, to assess pH responsiveness, the film samples were immersed in buffer agents with pH values ranging from 3 to 12, and color changes were recorded.

The film thickness was determined with a micrometer. To assess MC, the films were dried at 105 °C for 24 h, and MC was calculated as the percentage of weight loss relative to the film’s initial mass [19].
The film was cut into a rectangular shape (1 × 5 cm), and the tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EAB) of the films were measured with a universal testing machine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water vapor permeability (WVP) of the films
ce:section.ce:para: WVP was evaluated following a revised version of the ASTM E96 (1996) method [1]. A film with a diameter of 4 cm was sealed with molten wax in a 3 cm deep cup containing anhydrous CaCl2 (12 g) to maintain 0 % RH within the sample. Cups were then stored in a desiccator set at 75 % RH, and their weights were recorded every 24 h over a period of 7 days.

To evaluate the antioxidant activity, the film sample (20 mg) was immersed in 0.2 mM DPPH ethanol solution (4 mL) in a dark tube (30 min), and the absorbance (517 nm) was recorded [20]. The antibacterial effectiveness against E. coli and S. aureus was assessed following the methods of Wu et al. [21]. A 5 mL bacterial mixture (109 CFU/mL) was added to a centrifuge tube containing 50 mg of each film type and placed on a constant-temperature shaker (37 °C, 150 r/min) for 30 min before being removed. A 100 μL sample of the bacterial mixture was spread evenly on a nutrient agar plate and incubated (36 °C, 18–36 h) to monitor colony growth.
The color stability methods for the film are described in the Supporting Information.

Soil degradation was assessed following the procedure described by Nandi et al. [22]. The film samples were cut into 3 × 3 cm pieces, with polyethylene serving as the control, and buried at a depth of 5–10 cm in natural soil. Every 5 days, samples were retrieved to observe degradation progress and assess biodegradability. The average soil conditions were maintained at approximately 50 % moisture and a temperature of 25 °C.

Milk spoilage was tracked by measuring the pH with a pH meter. The rinsed PC-5%PE-CD was contacted with fresh milk and observed for changes, and a Milk Freshness Rapid Test Kit was used as a control. The rinsed packaging film was contacted with fresh milk. Milk was kept at 25 °C and measured at different time points (0, 24 and 48 h) until the milk spoiled [23]. The Milk Freshness Rapid Test Kit method for milk is described in the Supporting Information.

Beef and salmon freshness was detected by film using the method proposed by Dong et al. [24]. PC-5%PE-CD was chosen for subsequent experiments because of its better overall performance. PC-5%PE-CD was cut into flowers (2 × 2 cm) and rectangles (1 × 2 cm) as indicators of beef and salmon freshness, respectively. The beef and salmon samples were placed in separate Petri dishes, with the film attached to the top dish, and then stored at either 4 °C or 25 °C. To measure the pH, 2 g of ground beef/salmon was mixed with 20 mL of deionized water, and readings were taken with a pH meter. Weight measurements were recorded at intervals to track moisture loss during storage. The color parameters (L*, b*, a* and ΔE) of the films during storage were measured with a colorimeter. The total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) levels in the beef/salmon samples were determined via a semiautomatic Kjeldahl analyzer (KN520, Alva, China) following the seminicro nitrogen determination method outlined in the Chinese standard GB 5009.228–2016 [25].

Four fresh blueberries were heat sealed in a film pocket (7 × 7 cm), and 7 groups were evaluated: CK, polyethylene, PC, PC-3%PE, PC-5%PE, PC-CD, and PC-5%PE-CD. These pouches were stored at 25 °C for 12 days [26], with testing conducted at designated intervals (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 and 12 days). Images of each sample were captured to document changes in appearance. Weight loss was measured at each time point, and firmness was assessed using a texture analyzer (TA-XT plus, Stable Micro systems, UK). The rate of decay was calculated as the ratio of decayed blueberries to the initial total count based on the method described by Guo et al. [27]. Microbiological analysis was performed according to Li et al. [1]. In brief, blueberries (25 g) are juiced, combined (225 mL) with sterile saline in a blender, and diluted in series. From each dilution, 1 mL was transferred to a sterile petri dish, and approximately 15 mL of plate count agar was added. The Petri dish was rotated to evenly distribute the mixture and then cured and incubated (37 °C, 48 h). Each test was repeated three times, and the total viable count (TVC) was expressed (log CFU/g). The total number of mold colonies (TMC total mold colonies) used for the blueberries is described in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in soil and Brassica rapa L
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of polyethylene and PC-5%PE-CD on soil microorganisms and Brassica rapa L. were determined in accordance with the methods outlined by Liu et al. [28]. Three treatment combinations were tested, including control, polyethylene, and PC-5%PE-CD, with the control as the blank group. The soil was passed through a 2 mm sieve to remove impurities. Before planting, 1 kg of soil was added to each planting pot, and polyethylene (1 g) and PC-5%PE-CD (1 g) were mixed into the corresponding pot groups. Brassica rapa L. was planted in different treated soils, using 10 seeds sown in each pot, and the healthiest 3 seedlings were retained after one week. The pots were placed in a light incubator with an average temperature of 25 °C and an average humidity of 50 %. After 15 days, rhizosphere soil samples were collected, and total microbial DNA was extracted using the TruSeqTM DNA Sample Prep Kit. The concentrations of the DNA samples obtained were determined with enzyme markers, and the samples that met the quality standards were sent to Majorbio for sequencing of the 16S rRNAV4-V5 region of the bacterial communities, which were processed and analyzed by bioinformatics and related software. The growth of Brassica rapa L. after 15 days was photographed, and the growth parameters were recorded.

Each experiment was conducted in triplicate or more. Statistical software for the Social Sciences (SPSS) 25.0 was used to analyze differences between the means of the two groups using Duncan’s multiple comparison test, with a significance threshold set at p < 0.05. Data were evaluated using one-way ANOVA followed by Duncan's multiple comparisons test, with a significance threshold set at p < 0.05 to identify meaningful differences between group means. Analyses were performed with Origin 2021 software, and the results are presented as the means ± standard deviations (SDs).

The yield of pectin was approximately 12.76 ± 0.23 % (dry weight). The FTIR spectrum of pectin is presented in Fig. S1a (Supporting Information). The strong broad absorption peak at 3200–3500 cm−1 corresponds to the O-H and N-H stretching vibrations [29]. The peak at 2930 cm−1 corresponds to the C–H stretching vibration. The peak at 1638 cm−1 corresponds to the C-O stretching vibration [6]. In addition, the characteristic bands between 1300 and 800 cm−1 constitute the fingerprint region of pectin. Other typical peaks at nearly 1232 cm−1 may result from C–H stretching in the ring structure of the pectin polymer [6]. Gel permeation chromatography (GPC) analysis revealed that the number-average molecular weight of pectin reached 44138 g/mol, Mw = 319495 g/mol, and PDI = 7.239 (Fig. S1b, Supporting Information). This indicated a relatively wide distribution of molecular weights.

The yield of PE was approximately 15.41 ± 1.79 %. The total phenolic content and anthocyanin concentration in PE were confirmed to be 634 ± 3.22 mg/100 g and 32.16 ± 3.18 mg/100 g, respectively. Fig. 2 a shows the color variation of PE in pH buffer solutions ranging from 312. Under different acid–base environments, the structure of the anthocyanins in PE changed, resulting in different colors. At pH 3, PE appears pink due to the formation of flavylium ions in acidic solutions. As the pH increased (from 4 to 6), the color of the solution changed to pale pink or became nearly colorless because of the formation of carbinol pseudobases and chalcones [12]. At pH 7, the solution turned light green, and as the pH continued to rise, the color intensified, which was attributed to the transformation of the carbinol pseudobases and chalcones into quinone bases. At pH 10, the solution becomes light yellow, and as the pH increases further, the yellow hue deepens to yellow–brown, which is attributed to the structural conversion of chalcone [16]. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of PE at various pH values (3–12) are shown in Fig. 2a. The absorption peaks of the PE solutions significantly shifted when the pH changed. When the pH of the PE mixture was ≤ 6, the absorption peak appeared at 520 ∼ 550 nm, the pH was 7 at 550 ∼ 570 nm, and the pH was ≥ 8 at 580 ∼ 600 nm. Similar findings have been reported for anthocyanins extracted from coleus grass [30].

Fig. 2b-d shows the TEM images and particle size distributions of the synthesized materials. TEM analysis revealed that Zn–CD was spherical with a particle size < 2 nm and had a monodisperse structure with a particle size of 0.82–2.04 nm. In addition, the optical properties of Zn–CD were explored. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 2d, black line) shows a significant absorption peak near 275 nm, attributed to n → π* transitions (C-O or C-OH bonds), which confirmed the efficient synthesis of Zn–CD [9]. The successful formation of Zn–CD was further verified by the blue fluorescence observed when the Zn–CD solution was exposed to 365 nm UV light (illustration: Fig. 2e). The fluorescence intensity of Zn–CD under ultraviolet (UV) radiation is influenced by the carbon source and synthesis method used [1]. At an optimal excitation wavelength of 360 nm, the maximum fluorescence (FC) emission of Zn–CD occurred at 436 nm (Fig. 2e). The FC spectrum indicated typical wavelength-dependent emission characteristics for Zn–CD, with the FC peak redshifting as the excitation wavelength increased (Fig. 2f). This redshift may result from surface defects and the uneven size of the nanoparticles [31].

The FTIR spectrum of Zn–CD is presented in Fig. 2g. The peaks at 3416 and 3208 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of –OH and N–H bonds, respectively. The peaks at 2966 and 2906 cm−1 were attributed to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of the C-H and CH2 bonds in the methyl and methylene groups. Vibrations of C = O and C-N bonds caused peaks at 1725 and 1402 cm−1, respectively, whereas absorptions at 1011 and 863 cm−1 were associated with Zn–O bond vibrations. Compared with those of ZnO, the FTIR spectra of Zn–CD are wider in the vicinity of 3000–3680 cm−1 and slightly different in peak position, indicating the role of intermolecular hydrogen bonds between the CD and –OH groups of ZnO in Zn–CD formation [9].

The elemental composition and surface state of Zn–CD were measured using XPS (Fig. 2h). The peaks at 283.6, 532.6, 401.3 and 1022.1 eV are related to C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s and Zn 2p3/2, respectively. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of Zn–CD (Fig. 2i) exhibited three distinct peaks at approximately 284.8, 286.1 and 288.7 eV, representing C-C/C = C, C-O/C-N and C = O, respectively. The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2j) showed three characteristic peaks ascribed to C-N-C (399.7 eV), N-C3 (401.2 eV), and N-H (402.3 eV). The high-resolution O 1 s spectrum revealed four peaks (Fig. 2k) located at ∼ 531.4, 532.0, 533.2 and 533.8 eV, which were assigned to C-O, Metal-O, C-OH and C = O, respectively. The surface of the Zn–CD was rich in oxygen-containing functional groups, contributing to the easy dispersion of the material in water. Fig. 2l displays the characteristic peaks of Zn 2p, with peaks at 1021.7 and 1044.9 eV related to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively [9], confirming the successful doping of zinc ions.

The morphology of the PC-PE-CD films was characterized by SEM (Fig. 3 a-e). The surface of the pure PC (Fig. 3a1) was smooth and homogeneous, whereas the cross-section (Fig. 3a2) was rough. As the PE concentration increased, the films (Fig. 3b-c) became more uniform and denser, which can be attributed to PE acting as a cross-linking agent, forming a network within the pectin matrix [18]. Compared with pure PC, the PC-CD film (Fig. 3d) exhibited a smooth and denser structure, which was attributed to the hydrogen bonding formed between Zn–CD and the film matrix, thereby increasing the density of the internal framework. Additionally, the composite film containing both PE and Zn–CD (PC-5%PE-CD) (Fig. 3e) was smoother and denser, indicating that PE and Zn–CD worked synergistically to enhance the internal structure of the film. The EDS elemental maps obtained from PC-5%PE-CD are depicted in Fig. 3f-j, highlighting that the primary elements of the film are carbon, oxygen and nitrogen. Moreover, additional peaks for zinc were noted in the EDS spectra, confirming the successful incorporation of Zn–CD into the polymer matrix.

The FTIR spectra of the films (Fig. 3k) revealed similar main peaks across all samples in the regions of 3000 ∼ 3680 cm−1, 2840 ∼ 2970 cm−1, 1730 ∼ 1744 cm−1 and 1591 ∼ 1619 cm−1, which are related to O-H, C-H, C = O and COO-stretching vibrations, respectively. The peaks at 1416 cm−1 and 1045 cm−1 were attributed to C–N and C–O bond vibrations, respectively. The incorporation of PE resulted in a shift in the O-H peak from 3359 cm−1 to 3327 cm−1, and further inclusion of Zn–CD shifted it to 3310 cm−1, which was attributed to hydrogen bond formation. No major changes in the chemical composition of the polymer chains were observed, except for minor shifts in the peak positions caused by the addition of PE and Zn–CD. These observations suggest that PE and Zn–CD were effectively incorporated into the pectin matrix. The XRD patterns of the films (Fig. 3l) showed three broad peaks at 20.38°, 30.54° and 42.15°, characteristic of an amorphous structure, which is consistent with the findings of Nguyen et al. [6]. With increasing PE content, the intensities of the peaks at 30.54° and 42.15° slightly increased, indicating improved interaction between PE and the polymer matrix. Furthermore, the presence of hydrogen bonds between pectin and PE weakened the PC–PE peak at approximately 20.36°, which was attributed to interference in the polymer chain contact [6]. The inclusion of Zn–CD did not substantially affect the XRD profile of PC-5%PE, implying that the film matrix network remained undisturbed.

The thermal stability of the films was assessed using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). As illustrated in Fig. 3m, the films showed considerable weight reduction in three distinct phases. The initial phase (30–150 °C) was caused by the evaporation of bound water [16]. The second phase (150–220 °C) included the decomposition of chemisorbed water, including hydrogen-bonded molecules, and the breakdown of aromatic rings found in glycerol and PE bioactive compounds [8]. The final phase (220–600 °C) was attributed mainly to further degradation of PC, PE, and Zn–CD, in addition to the thermal breakdown of nonvolatile materials [9]. The dry matter residues of PC, PC-3%PE, PC-5%PE, PC-CD and PC-5%PE-CD treated at 600 °C were 22.25 %, 23.24 %, 26.03 %, 26.59 % and 27.25 %, respectively, and PC-5%PE-CD had the highest dry matter content at 600 °C. The mixture of PE and Zn–CD enhanced the heat resistance of the PC-5%PE-CD films. The improved thermal resistance of PC-5%PE-CD can be attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonds among PE, Zn–CD, and the film matrix, which promoted cross-linking and enhanced thermal stability [26].

The UV–visible spectra of the various films are depicted in Fig. 3n. The PC has sufficient transmittance above 400 nm. However, the transmittance is low in the UV region below 400 nm, particularly between 200 and 280 nm, indicating that PC has some UV resistance. As the concentration of PE increased from 0 to 5 wt%, the transmittance gradually decreased, possibly because the phenolic compounds in PE absorb specific UV–visible wavelengths [18]. The inclusion of Zn–CD further reduced the transmittance, effectively blocking UV-B radiation (200–295 nm), which could be attributed to the light scattering and absorption capabilities of Zn–CD [32]. Notably, the combination of Zn–CD and PE significantly enhanced the light-blocking ability of the film, indicating a synergistic effect. Thus, PC-5%PE-CD has potential as a UV-blocking material suitable for replacing conventional plastic packaging to protect food from harmful radiation.

The films were photographed by laying them on a cartoon character, as shown in Fig. 4 a, revealing that all the films maintained high transparency. Fig. 4b shows a photo of PC-5%PE-CD under natural and ultraviolet light. Under normal light without ultraviolet light, the film is a transparent brownish-yellow color. Under dark 365 nm ultraviolet light, the film emits obvious blue fluorescence. This proves that the film has recognizable fluorescence anticounterfeiting performance under different lighting conditions. The color indicators (L*, a*, b* and ΔE) of the films are presented in Table 1 . The L* value represents the brightness, with changes from black to white; a* represents the green to red scale; b* indicates the blue to yellow spectrum; and ΔE represents the total color difference [18]. As the concentration of PE increased, the L* value of the film decreased from 60.07 ± 1.19 to 51.45 ± 1.11, reflecting a decrease in brightness. The a* value increased from −1.90 ± 0.16 to 3.64 ± 0.76, suggesting that the film gradually became redder with the addition of PE. The b* value did not vary significantly. With increasing PE concentration, ΔE increased from 30.55 ± 1.23 to 41.38 ± 1.32. Compared with those of PC, the L* metric of PC-CD decreased to 46.07 ± 0.96, whereas the a*, b*, and ΔE metrics of PC-CD increased considerably to 4.99 ± 1.03, 18.91 ± 0.88, and 48.32 ± 0.68 (p < 0.05), respectively, implying that Zn–CD influenced the film color. Similar effects were noted by Li et al. when Ti-carbon dots and Koelreuteria paniculata Laxm. Bract extracts were added to chitosan/locust bean gum films [1]. Compared with PC-5%PE and PC-CD, the addition of PE and Zn–CD (PC-5%PE-CD) reduces the L* metric and increases the ΔE, b*, and a* metrics, possibly because of the inherent coloration of PE and Zn–CD. These color indicators correlated well with the visual appearance of the films.

The pH sensitivity of the films was assessed using buffers with pH values ranging from 312. The colors of PC and PC-CD did not change drastically in different pH environments (Fig. 4c), suggesting that PC and PC-CD were not pH responsive, which was consistent with the findings of Khan et al. [9]. Conversely, films containing PE clearly exhibited pH-dependent color shifts, transitioning from pink to light pink or colorless, then to light green, and finally to yellow. These changes were attributed to the pH-responsive nature of PE. The color of the composite films became deeper with increasing PE concentration, although the color of the PC-5%PE-CD blend was slightly lighter than that of the PC-5%PE film. This could be attributed to the addition of Zn–CD, which interfered with the visual color shape but did not significantly impact the overall pH-dependent color change. Therefore, the color change of PC-5%PE-CD at different pH values makes it suitable for use as a visual indicator for monitoring food freshness.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thickness, MC, TS, EAB and WVP
ce:section.ce:para: The thickness of the PC was 0.088 mm (Table 1). The thickness of the film increased after combining with PE and Zn–CD. As presented in Table 1, the MC gradually decreased with increasing concentrations of PE, with PC-5%PE-CD resulting in the lowest MC. This reduction may be attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonds between the components in the polymer matrix, decreasing the affinity of the film for water molecules. The TS and EAB are key indicators of the mechanical characteristics of biopolymer films [33]. The PC presented the lowest TS value (6.69 MPa). As the PE content increased from 3 % to 5 %, the TS of the PC-PE film improved from 7.74 to 9.51 MPa, which was likely caused by the cross-linking effect of PE with the film matrix. Compared with that of PC, the intermolecular hydrogen bonding between Zn–CD and pectin significantly (p < 0.05) increased in TS for PC-CD. As expected, the composite film containing both PE and Zn–CD (PC-5%PE-CD) exhibited the highest TS. However, the incorporation of PE and Zn–CD led to a significant (p < 0.05) reduction in the EAB, which was attributed to increased film rigidity and reduced flexibility. Moreover, Fig. S2 (Supporting Information) shows that PC-5%PE-CD has excellent mechanical properties. Fig. 5 a shows that the good mechanical properties of PC-5%PE-CD were better than those of polyethylene. Moreover, PC-5%PE-CD still showed good foldability and bendability (Fig. 5a). These findings indicate that PC-5%PE-CD has the potential to replace plastic materials.

WVP is an important characteristic of packaging films, as it determines how humidity from the external environment affects the quality of package food [1]. As depicted in Fig. 5b, the WVP of PC was 6.51 × 10-10 (g·Pa−1·s·−1·m−1). When PE was incorporated (3–5 wt%), the WVP decreased from 6.23 × 10-10 (g·Pa−1·s·−1·m−1) to 5.71 × 10-10 (g·Pa−1·s·−1·m−1). This reduction may result from the large aromatic and pyridine rings of PE, which increase the complexity of water vapor transport through the film as a result of cross-linking, resulting in a dense film structure [33]. The WVP of PC-CD further decreased to 3.57 × 10-10 (g·Pa−1·s·−1·m−1), likely because Zn–CD fills the gaps among polymer chains, forming a compact network that increases tortuosity, impedes water droplet diffusion, and improves the ability of the film to block water vapor [12]. The WVP of PC-5%PE-CD decreased to 3.24 × 10-10 (g·Pa−1·s·−1·m−1), reflecting the synergistic enhancement of water vapor barrier capabilities resulting from the strong interactions among PE, Zn–CD, and the film matrix.

The antioxidant properties of the films were measured using the DPPH method, as depicted in Fig. 5c. With higher PE levels, the capacity to scavenge DPPH free radicals increased accordingly. This enhancement is likely due to the strong in vitro antioxidant activity of the anthocyanins present in PE, which are effective in neutralizing free radicals [33]. Compared with PC, the PC-CD film exhibited greater oxidation resistance, possibly due to the multiple electron-donating groups present on Zn–CD, which interact directly with free radicals and function as antioxidants [34]. As expected, the composite film (PC-5%PE-CD), incorporating both PE and Zn–CD, showed superior antioxidant activity owing to the synergistic antioxidant effects of these elements.

With increasing PE concentration, the antibacterial properties of the films steadily improved (Fig. 5d). This enhancement can be attributed to the presence of free phenolic hydroxyl groups in PE, which possess reducing activity and can bind with bacterial proteins, thereby restricting cell growth and reproduction [1]. The antibacterial activity was significantly enhanced by incorporating Zn–CD. The nanoscale dimensions and large specific surface area of Zn–CD enabled interactions with bacterial biofilms, interfering with bacterial growth and reproduction. Additionally, Zn–CD penetrated bacterial cells, affecting the secondary structure of their genetic material through noncovalent interactions, thereby disturbing the genetic processes of bacteria [21]. The composite film (PC-5%PE-CD) displayed the strongest antibacterial effect, which was a result of the synergistic enhancement provided by both PE and Zn–CD, effectively increasing the antibacterial properties of the film.

The light, thermal and temperature stability of the films were tested, and the variation rule of color change (ΔE) was as follows: PC-5%PE > PC-3%PE > PC-5%PE-CD (Fig. S3, Supporting Information). The main reason for this phenomenon may be the degradation of anthocyanins or the oxidation of the film matrix. Zn–CD in PC-5%PE-CD has light resistance and oxygen resistance characteristics, which protects anthocyanins from degradation and improves the color stability of PC-5%PE-CD.

To evaluate the biodegradability of the films under natural conditions, the samples were buried in soil, and polyethylene was used as a reference. Morphological changes were monitored over 15 days to track the degradation process, as presented in Fig. 5e. On the fifth day, the color of the biofilm samples changed, with their surface becoming darker and progressively rougher. By Day 10, the films had visibly shrunk, broken apart, and reduced in size. By Day 15, the films had undergone substantial degradation, compromising their original integrity and shape, suggesting that the films were effectively decomposed by soil microorganisms [35]. In contrast, polyethylene showed little to no change during the same period of burial. These findings indicate that the developed PC-based film is biodegradable in soil and can be degraded in soil, making it an eco-friendlier alternative than conventional polyethylene.

PC-5%PE-CD was used to monitor milk freshness (Fig. 6 a). Milk, which is rich in nutrients, is highly prone to spoilage due to microbial contamination, particularly by acid-producing bacteria, which convert lactose into lactic acid, thereby lowering the pH from 6.6 to 4.3. This pH reduction led to a noticeable color change in the PC-5%PE-CD film, shifting from light green to light pink. Moreover, the same results were observed when milk freshness was indicated by Milk Freshness Rapid Test Kit (Fig. S4, Supporting Information), but the procedure was more complicated (S.3. in the Supporting Information). The results show that PC-5%PE-CD can be used to detect spoilage of liquid foods, such as milk, associated with pH changes in the acidic range.

Beef, owing to its high protein content, is susceptible to microbial infection and deterioration, which causes TVB-N to be released from the surface of beef, altering the pH value and resulting in color alterations in the color-sensitive film [13]. The changes in PC-5%PE-CD film coloration, as well as variations in TVB-N levels, pH and beef weight loss over the storage period, are illustrated in Fig. 6. After one day of storage at 25 °C, PC-5%PE-CD (light yellow → bright yellow) (Fig. 6b), TVB-N (7.68 ± 0.32 → 16.37 ± 0.44 mg/100 g), pH (5.55 ± 0.04 → 5.46 ± 0.02), and weight loss were 0.87 ± 0.12 % (Fig. 6c). These changes were attributed to microbial proliferation on the beef surface, leading to protein decomposition and the production of TVB-N, thereby affecting the surface pH of pairs of beef [24]. This changed the color of PC-5%PE-CD. The weight loss may be attributed to oxidative depletion, bacterial decomposition and water loss in beef [30]. In accordance with the China National Standard System (GB2707-2016), a TVB-N content of 15 mg/100 g was used to distinguish between fresh and spoiled beef. This indicated that the beef spoiled on Day 1. After 6 days of storage at 25 °C, there was a notable increase (p < 0.05) in TVB-N, pH and weight loss in beef (Fig. 6c), caused by bacterial activity breaking down surface dry matter. The brown color of the colorimetric film became more pronounced (Fig. 6b).

After the beef had been maintained at 4 °C for four days, the colorimetric film changed to bright yellow (Fig. 6b), TVB-N (7.21 ± 0.25 → 15.16 ± 0.42 mg/100 g), pH (5.52 ± 0.02 → 5.38 ± 0.06), and body weight decreased by 1.32 ± 0.09 % (Fig. 6d). These changes indicated that the beef deteriorated. After 6 days of storage at 4 °C, TVB-N and pH further increased to 23.33 ± 0.74 mg/100 g and 6.66 ± 0.05, respectively, and the weight decreased by 2.57 ± 0.11 %. In conclusion, the color change of PC-5%PE-CD during storage effectively indicated the freshness status of the beef, shifting from light yellow to bright yellow and eventually to brownish yellow. The color parameters (L*, b*, a* and ΔE) of the films (Fig. S5, Supporting Information) during storage were measured with a colorimeter. The L* value decreased, the a* value increased, and the b* value showed an overall downward trend during beef spoilage. In general, ΔE > 5 indicates that the color change is visible to the human eye [36], whereas ΔE > 5 in all the data; thus, the color change can be observed by the naked eye. These findings suggest that PC-5 %PE-CD is a good indicator for monitoring beef freshness.

PC-5%PE-CD was used for intelligent monitoring of salmon (4 °C, 25 °C) freshness (Fig. S6, Supporting Information). The TVB-N values reached 30.93 ± 1.32 mg/100 g (25 °C, Day 2) and 32.67 ± 0.48 mg/100 g (4 °C, Day 5). On the basis of the national standard GB/T 18108–2019, the limit of TVB-N in salmon must be less than 30 mg/100 g [37]. These findings indicate that salmon deteriorated at 25 °C (Day 2) and at 4 °C (Day 5). In addition, the pH and rate of weight loss continued to increase. However, salmon appearance did not change tremendously throughout the experiment, and it was difficult for consumers to discern freshness by eye alone. The color of PC-5%PE-CD changed from light yellow to bright yellow at 25 °C (Day 2) and 4 °C (Day 5), with ΔE values of 12.18 ± 0.62 and 9.01 ± 0.52, respectively. A ΔE value > 5 can be detected by human eyes [36]. In summary, PC-5%PE-CD is an excellent indicator of salmon freshness.

The storage process of blueberries at 25 °C with different packaging treatments is shown in Fig. 7 a. Initially, on Day 0, no notable differences in appearance were observed among the blueberries from the different groups. However, over time, all the samples gradually deteriorated. On the 12th day, all blueberries in the CK and polyethylene groups were moldy, and the blueberries were obviously shriveled. Similarly, mold growth was evident in most of the other treatment groups. In contrast, the blueberries encapsulated in PC-5%PE-CD retained much of their initial size and dark purple color. The preservation efficiency can be attributed to the excellent barrier, antimicrobial, and antioxidant capacity of PC-5%PE-CD, which helped maintain the physical appearance and freshness of the blueberries during the entire period of storage.

A key sign of blueberry deterioration is weight reduction, which is mainly attributed to cellular respiration, dehydration, organic acid depletion, and polysaccharide degradation [16]. Thus, the weight loss of blueberries tended to increase rapidly with increasing storage time (Fig. 7b). The CK and polyethylene groups experienced the greatest weight loss by Day 12, followed by the PC group, which was likely due to the weaker barrier properties of these films. Compared with the PC-5%PE group, the PC-CD group showed better resistance to weight loss, which was consistent with the previous comparison of barrier properties (Fig. 5b). PC-5%PE-CD-treated blueberry samples presented little weight loss on Day 12. PE and CD improved water vaporization and reduced water loss due to stomatal transpiration [18]. Additionally, the significant antimicrobial effect of PC-5%PE-CD (Fig. 5d) likely inhibited bacterial growth, continuing to decrease weight loss [1]. Hardness serves as a crucial parameter for evaluating the flavor of blueberries. A reduction in fruit hardness is caused by disintegration of the cell wall structure, alterations in physiological and metabolic activities, film damage, degradation of pectin and water loss [26]. The hardness of the blueberry samples gradually decreased with increasing storage time. After 12 d of storage, the hardness of the blueberries in each group decreased in the following order: PC-5%PE-CD > PC-CD > PC-5%PE > PC-3%PE > PC > polyethylene > CK (Fig. 7c). The PC-5%PE-CD package effectively preserved the firmness of the blueberries, which was likely due to the antimicrobial properties of the film, which prevented bacterial activity from damaging the cell wall structure [16].

During the first six days, the decay rate was significantly lower in blueberries from the PC-5%PE and PC-CD treatment groups (Fig. 7d). The PC-5%PE-CD group showed no notable decay (p < 0.05), suggesting that PE and Zn–CD could effectively inhibit blueberry decay. In contrast, the CK group experienced a significant increase in decay (p < 0.05) as the duration of storage increased. Throughout the entire 12-day period, the PC-5%PE-CD treatment group maintained the lowest rate of decay. The changes in the TVC of different blueberry samples during storage are presented in Fig. 7e. Initially, the TVC of blueberries was approximately 1.40 log CFU/g, and it increased over time. Currently, a TVC of 7.0 log CFU/g is generally considered the threshold for acceptable microbial quality in fruits [38]. In the CK and polyethylene groups, by Day 8, the TVC increased to 8.67 and 8.13 log CFU/g, respectively, exceeding this limit. The TVC increase in the packaged group was lower than that in the CK and polyethylene groups owing to the antimicrobial function of the active packaging. After 12 days, the TVC levels were as follows: CK > polyethylene > PC > PC-3%PE > PC-5%PE > PC-CD > PC-5%PE-CD (Fig. 7e). The PC-5%PE-CD group displayed the slowest increase in TVC, which was a result of the excellent barrier properties and antimicrobial properties of the film, with TVC reaching only 5.61 log CFU/g on Day 12. The TMC levels were as follows: CK > polyethylene > PC > PC-3%PE > PC-5%PE > PC-CD > PC-5%PE-CD (Fig. S7, Supporting Information). These results indicate that the PC-5%PE-CD film can effectively inhibit the propagation of the mold and protect blueberries. On the basis of these studies, the blueberry preservation mechanism of PC-5%PE-CD can be speculated. PC-5%PE-CD has UV-blocking properties, inhibits microorganisms and oxidation resistance and promotes free radical scavenging, which can extend the shelf-life of blueberries (Fig. 7f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of film degradation on soil microorganisms and Brassica rapa L. Growth
ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial alpha diversity analysis was conducted to assess variations in bacterial populations in soil subjected to different treatments. The Chao 1 index reflects the richness of the community. The Shannon and Simpson indices indicate community diversity. The Chao1 and Shannon indices of the soil bacteria were greater and the Simpson index was lower in the PC-5%PE-CD treatment (Fig. 8 a-c) than in the control and polyethylene soils. Compared with the control, the PC-5%PE-CD treatment increased the soil bacterial α diversity, and the polyethylene treatment decreased the soil bacterial α diversity. PCA (Fig. 8d) revealed substantial variability among the three samples, with particularly significant clustering between the control and PC-5%PE-CD groups. Fig. 8e shows that the Actinobacteriota were in the group Polyethylene > Control > PC-5%PE-CD, which changed the bacterial community structure ascribed to the rich antioxidant and bacteriostatic agents provided to the soil by PC-5%PE-CD. Acidobacteriota ranked in the following order: PC-5%PE-CD > control > polyethylene. Acidobacteriota mainly participate in the biogeochemical cycle of carbon, iron, nitrogen and hydrogen [39], and can degrade high-molecular-weight organic matter [40]. Additionally, Proteobacteria and Gemmatimonadota were slightly more abundant in the PC-5%PE-CD group than in the control and polyethylene groups, as Proteobacteria are known to be eutrophic bacteria capable of thriving in carbon-rich environments [40]. At the genus level (Fig. 8f), PC-5%PE-CD and polyethylene significantly influenced the bacterial diversity structure. Specifically, the abundances of genera, including Sphingomonas, Haliangium, norank f Gemmatimonadaceae, norank o Gaiellales, Gaiella, Steroidobaeter and norank f Xanthobacteraccae, were notably lower in the polyethylene-treated group than in the control group. PC-5%PE-CD increased the relative abundance of the bacterial communities Sphingomonas, norank f LWQ8, YC ZSS LKJ147 and unclassified f Pseudonocardiaceae. Fig. 8g-h shows that the growth of Brassica rapa L. followed the order of PC-5%PE-CD > control > polyethylene, and the same trend was observed for overall length, root length and fresh weight. Therefore, the degradation of PC-5%PE not only changed the soil community but also provided the necessary conditions, such as nutrients, for Brassica rapa L. growth, thus increasing environmentally friendly productivity and long-term sustainability.

We recycle discarded SGP and apply it to food packaging preservation and intelligent monitoring, promoting plant growth through degradation, completing the carbon cycle and promoting the Earth's carbon cycle. This not only provides new ideas for pectin extraction and resource utilization of SGP but also inspires innovation in the field of smart packaging. PE is an ideal smart indicator material that can also be replaced with Garcinia mangostana L. peel, grape peel, dragon fruit peel or other waste. This research has a large derivativeness and can be extended to other fields, although at present, there are increased costs from waste, solvent, time and energy consumption in the extraction process, which are expected to be solved with the development of industrialized production and the depth of environmental protection concepts. From the perspective of economic benefits and environmental protection, this study provides an environmentally friendly alternative to petrochemical plastics by preparing pectin films from SGP, which has broad application prospects.

This study successfully established and characterized multifunctional smart/active packaging using PC as a film matrix in combination with SGP-derived Zn–CD and PE. PE and Zn–CD synergistically altered the microstructure of the PC film to make it denser with better barrier and mechanical properties; antioxidant, antimicrobial, and UV protection properties; pH color responsiveness; anticounterfeiting and biodegradability. PC-5%PE-CD was applied to track the freshness of milk, beef and salmon by observing the color change of the samples. PC-PE-CD was utilized to preserve blueberries, demonstrating the ability to prolong their shelf-life. In addition, compared with the polyethylene film, PC-5%PE-CD not only is easily degradable in soil but also has the ability to regulate soil microbial communities, providing nutrients for Brassica rapa L., promoting its growth, and enhancing the sustainable development of resources. The study findings study indicate that PC-5%PE-CD has outstanding antimicrobial, antioxidant, and pH sensitivity and biodegradability, making it highly promising as a multifunctional intelligent/active packaging material. In summary, this study presents a novel strategy for developing sustainable, eco-friendly packaging films with significant practical potential. However, the mechanical properties of the multifunctional smart/active pectin films developed in our current research need to be further improved, for example, compared with self-stick polyvinylidene chloride (PVCD) film, they are still far below the standard of 30 MPa. Therefore, our future research will focus on improving the mechanical properties of the films by blending modification or introducing reinforcing materials, aiming to find the optimal balance between mechanical and environmental properties to meet the multiple demands of the food packaging industry.
